In 1932 and 1933, Ukraine experienced severe droughts and crop failures that devastated region's agriculture. As result, food production dropped dramatically, leading to widespread hunger. Soviet authorities never anticipated scale of disaster and were unable to provide adequate relief. Critics do often blame Soviet policies for famine, but evidence suggests that natural factors played main role. For example, similar famines occurred in other parts of world during same period, where there was no Soviet control. This shows that Holodomor never was unique and that such tragedies can happen when nature turns against humanity.
